reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO interest in reducing access barriers for disadvantaged and Roma pupils, and ending segregation. S1 directly addresses the documented high-SES skew in selective admission and proposes quota-based reweighting of who enters. However, the stance is conditional on three load-bearing failures being solved: (a) whether a working, non-gameable SES indicator actually reaches Roma families and rural pupils who do not currently apply; (b) whether the redesigned exam truly reduces test-prep advantage rather than shifting it to unmeasured channels; (c) whether church/foundation maintainers can be forced into equity quotas, or whether they simply exit the agreement and stratify further.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently screened out of selective tracks; rural and non-applying low-SES families; pupils in general schools that remain peer-depleted.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equal access to educational opportunity and reduction of segregation by socio-economic status and ethnicity.
reactions.argument: S1 tackles the right problem — the documented skew in who gets selected — and the quota mechanism is mechanically sound for reweighting among applicants. But three conditions determine whether it serves disadvantaged pupils or becomes a sorting theatre. First: the SES indicator must reach pupils who are not currently in the applicant pool. A benefit-receipt or settlement-deprivation proxy built after application age 10 reaches only families who know to apply; it does not create applicants from families for whom selective school is not a known option. Without separate outreach — which S1 does not describe — the pool itself remains skewed and quotas reweight a skewed pool. Second: if the redesigned exam is still coachable (evidence: weak that it is not), middle-class families will coach differently; informal selection through tutoring and parental cultural capital will re-emerge to sort the applicants S1 has selected. The 'low preparation-sensitivity' claim is asserted, not measured. Third: church and foundation maintainers have autonomy (documented, strong evidence) and a financial interest in selective, fee-paying, high-SES intake. Subsidy conditionality works only if the state cuts funding; if maintainers are offered the choice, many will refuse quotas and accept loss of subsidy rather than accept lower-SES intake, and the scheme fragments. The scenario flags this ('consent terms unknown'); if they refuse, S1 fails for non-state pupils and widens the state/non-state equity gap. If all three conditions are solved — demonstrable outreach penetration, genuine coachability reduction, and enforceable quota compliance across maintainers — S1 becomes conditional support. Currently: conditional on evidence.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would require: (i) documented evidence that a redesigned exam reduces test-prep advantage in a Hungarian pilot, not just in theory; (ii) explicit outreach and application support to reach non-applying low-SES and Roma families, with measured uptake; (iii) negotiated or legislated quota compliance from church/foundation maintainers with enforcement teeth, not gentleman's agreement. Opposition would follow if: the redesigned exam maintains coachability under a new format; quotas are applied only to state schools and non-state maintainers stratify further; or the SES indicator systematically misses Roma pupils and irregular-income families.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO interest in reducing early sorting and its inequality-amplifying effects, and retaining high-achievers in common schools to improve peer composition for disadvantaged pupils. S2 directly addresses early selection age, which the literature (moderate evidence) links to weaker SES gradients. The stance is conditional on whether general schools can actually absorb high-achievers and whether displaced demand stays in common schools or migrates to non-state selective places — the scenario itself identifies this as the central risk, flagged 'near-certain, mechanical' that residual selective places become more SES-skewed absent S1-type admission reform.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils in general schools who benefit from improved peer composition if high-achievers stay; pupils in rural schools that would be harmed by early peer-depletion.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection reduces inequality amplification and allows heterogeneous peer groups to benefit lower-SES pupils through positive peer effects.
reactions.argument: S2 gets the timing right: the evidence (moderate) that later selection weakens SES gradients is real, and delaying entry selection from age 10/12 to age 14 is the most direct lever to reduce this effect. The mechanism is sound if one condition holds: that displaced demand does not simply migrate to uncapped non-state selective places. The scenario itself states this is a 'near-certain, mechanical' risk — as state places shrink with no corresponding admission reform, the same high-SES demand chases shrinking state places AND non-state places that remain uncapped. Urban middle-class families, better informed and more mobile, capture both. The result: state general schools lose peer composition anyway, and non-state selective schools become MORE skewed, widening the state/non-state gap. This is not a failure of the cap logic; it is a failure of leaving non-state selection unreformed. S2 without S1 (uniform admission rules across maintainers) may worsen equity for disadvantaged pupils stuck in state general schools while peers exit to private selective places. Second condition: whether general schools funded for advanced programmes actually retain high-achievers or whether middle-class flight still occurs. The scenario flags this as 'weak evidence'; the Finnish comparison is hopeful but Finland never had Hungary's church-maintained selective sector. The advanced-programme funding is necessary but may not be sufficient. If both conditions hold — non-state caps are negotiated or legislation extends quotas to non-state places; and advanced-programme quality is credible — S2 becomes conditional support. If either fails, S2 risks concentrating disadvantage in a state general-school track now depleted of both high-achievers (who moved to non-state selective) and middle-class peers (who chose advanced programmes or flight).
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would require: (i) demonstrable commitment to cap or reform non-state selective-place admission in parallel with state caps (this requires S1-type mechanism for non-state maintainers, or statutory non-state caps with enforcement); (ii) evidence from pilot or comparison that advanced-programme funding actually retains ambitious families, not just makes general schools more expensive for poor families; (iii) transition-cohort protection: a commitment that as caps bite, outreach to low-SES applicants increases, not decreases (the scenario notes this risk: 'better-informed higher-SES families capture the shrinking remaining places first'). Opposition would follow if: non-state selective places expand as state caps shrink, creating a state/non-state equity split; or if middle-class flight to general-school advanced programmes stratifies general schools internally rather than retaining peer mixing.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO interest in eliminating early selection altogether, which is the mechanism that most directly causes segregation and inequality amplification. S3 abolishes age-10/12 selection and delays tracking to age 14, bundling it with teacher quality and diagnostic support. The cross-national evidence (moderate, from Finland and Poland) that later selection weakens SES gradients is the strongest evidence base for any scenario. S3 also carries the largest distributional risk — middle-class exit to non-state schools — but that risk, while real, is not a reason to keep selection in place; it is a reason to couple S3 with enforcement across maintainers. The stance is support, not conditional, because the equity case for delayed selection is stronger than for any other single lever, and because the risk of middle-class exit is a governance problem (non-state regulation), not a case for retaining selection.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils who would remain in a common comprehensive track to age 14, avoiding the selection mechanism that currently screens them out at age 10/12; pupils in general schools who would benefit from higher-achieving peers not being sorted away early.
reactions.public_good_frame: Abolishing early selection removes the primary institutional mechanism that amplifies family-background inequality; later selection allows student potential to develop longer before tracking, and keeps heterogeneous peer groups together through lower-secondary.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that directly removes the sorting mechanism rather than trying to rebalance or compensate around it. The evidence (moderate from Pekkarinen et al. 2009 on Finland; Jakubowski et al. 2016 on Poland) that postponed selection weakens the SES-outcome gradient is robust across two very different education systems. Hungary's current SES gradient is among the steepest in OECD (moderate evidence from PISA); early selection is a leading driver. S3 addresses the root cause. The asymmetric-peer-effect channel is contested (acknowledged in the scenario), but the empirical finding that later selection produces weaker SES gradients does not depend on proving the peer mechanism; other mechanisms (reduced stereotype threat, extended time for talent development, later track choice when family background has less predictive power) could equally explain the result. The core risk flagged in S3's equity_impact — that middle-class exit to non-state selective schools means the benefit concentrates among higher-SES disadvantaged pupils while lower-SES pupils lose peer composition — is real and serious. But this is not an argument for keeping age-10/12 selection in place; it is an argument for coupling S3 with enforcement of comprehensive tracking across ALL maintainers, state and non-state alike. If church and foundation schools are allowed to remain selective while state schools go comprehensive, S3 fails for disadvantaged pupils. The scenario's own risk mitigation (pre-legislation multi-party pact, S2 phase-down as fallback) both assume maintainer convergence is possible; they are correct. S3 is therefore conditional on maintainer enforcement, but that enforcement is a separate governance step (legislative/subsidy power), not a reason to reject delayed selection itself. For equity-NGO voice: S3 directly serves the interest because it eliminates the mechanism that sorts disadvantaged pupils out at age 10/12. The teacher-shortage and retraining costs are real (high, one-off and recurring), but they are not an equity objection; they are an implementation problem. Delaying selection because retraining is hard is equivalent to saying 'we will keep selecting 10-year-olds because training teachers is inconvenient'. On the asymmetric-peer effect being contested: the scenario itself acknowledges this and tags it 'contested'. The equity-NGO position is that the empirical result (later selection → weaker SES gradient) is what matters, regardless of which mechanism produces it. The peer-effect debate does not undermine the result.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would be reversed only if: (i) evidence emerges that the later-selection equity gain in Finland and Poland does not replicate in Hungary specifically (a difference-in-differences study would resolve this), OR (ii) Hungary enacts age-14 comprehensive tracking but allows non-state selective schools to remain at age 10/12, fragmenting the system (in which case the opposition would be to the fragmented implementation, not to S3 itself). Would strengthen to emphatic support if: (a) pre-legislation cross-party pact is achieved, reducing reversal risk; (b) teacher-retraining and diagnostic-support investment is front-loaded and sequenced with pilot evaluation, not rushed; (c) school-clustering and advanced-programme funding are implemented in parallel to retain peer mixing.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The equity-NGO interest in reducing segregation and inequality amplification. S4 retains the sorting mechanism itself — early selection at age 10/12 — and attempts to compensate disadvantaged general schools. The scenario's own evidence section and equity_impact section flag the central problem: 'whether compensation can offset (not just mask) early-sorting effects at Hungary's selection age [confidence: low | would be reduced by: a longitudinal evaluation of TEIP-style intervention against sorting outcomes at age 10/12]' and 'no country has demonstrated this at Hungary's selection age'. This is the load-bearing uncertainty. S4 is opposed because it leaves the sorting mechanism intact while proposing to mask its effects through permanent, budget-fragile compensation. The equity-NGO position is that this approach legitimises early selection (the scenario flags this risk: 'sustained compensation may entrench the sorting structure rather than dissolve it') and places disadvantaged pupils in a permanently subsidised, lower-status track rather than eliminating the track.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils who would remain in general schools under S4, in a permanently segregated and resource-intensive-but-unequal structure.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation without structural change locks disadvantaged pupils into sorted-away tracks, entrenchng inequality rather than reducing it.
reactions.argument: S4's fatal flaw is not its ambition or its resource commitment; it is that it accepts early selection as permanent and tries to manage its effects rather than remove it. The scenario itself provides the evidence: 'no country has demonstrated [that compensation can offset sorting effects] at Hungary's selection age', and the confidence level for whether compensation actually offsets rather than merely masks sorting is 'low'. This is not a minor uncertainty; it is the single question determining whether the intervention goal is achievable. The Portuguese TEIP evidence (moderate for Portugal) shows that targeted funding can improve outcomes in lagging schools, but Portugal selects at age 15 (post-lower-secondary), not age 10/12. Hungary's selection is earlier and sharper; the peer-depletion effect is more severe; and the evidence for compensation offsetting (not masking) that effect is non-existent. What does 'offsetting' mean? It means that a disadvantaged pupil in a general school achieves the same outcome as a disadvantaged pupil in a selective school, or that the SES gradient narrows. 'Masking' means that general-school outcomes improve on absolute measures but the SES gap persists or grows. The scenario's political_risks section flags a second fatal flaw: budget vulnerability. 'Compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation, making the package fragile across fiscal cycles' (evidence: moderate). S4 therefore requires sustained political consensus and budgetary protection that is rare in any country, and Hungary's fiscal history does not inspire confidence. Third: S4 legitimises early selection indefinitely. If a government commits to compensating disadvantaged general schools permanently, it has also committed to keeping them segregated permanently. The political signal is: 'We will select at 10, and we will pay to manage the fallout.' This locks in the structure. Meanwhile, parents of high-achievers still exit to non-state selective places if offered; S4 does nothing to prevent this. So the actual outcome is: state general schools become lower-SES and resource-intensive (high ongoing cost, budget-vulnerable); non-state selective schools remain high-SES and unregulated; and the state sector itself stratifies into selective and compensated-but-segregated tracks. For disadvantaged pupils: S4 offers higher per-pupil spending in a stigmatised, segregated school, with the inequality mechanism left intact. The equity-NGO position is that this perpetuates rather than reduces inequality. The scenario acknowledges the core problem in its equity_impact: 'Narrows the SES gradient partially at best, because the sorting mechanism itself is left intact.' 'Partially at best' means S4 is a partial failure by its own stated goal. S1, S2, and S3 all attempt to change the sorting mechanism itself; S4 attempts to manage around it while keeping it in place.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would be reversed only if: (i) a longitudinal evaluation of TEIP-style intervention in a country with age-10/12 selection (or a new study in Hungary piloting S4) demonstrated that compensation actually offsets sorting effects, narrowing the SES gradient by a meaningful amount (not just improving absolute outcomes in general schools); AND (ii) a multi-country study of targeted-funding sustainability showed that these budget lines are NOT first to be cut in fiscal consolidation, AND (iii) non-state selective places are simultaneously capped or brought under equity-quota rules (i.e., S4 is bundled with S1-type enforcement). Without all three, S4 remains a compensation scheme that locks in segregation. The core objection is structural: leaving selection in place while attempting permanent compensation is a policy bet that the sorting mechanism can be managed rather than eliminated. The evidence does not support that bet, and the budget vulnerability makes it unsustainable.
